does: This paper introduces Gradient-Guided Reward Optimization (GGRO), an inference-time alignment method that steers LLM generation by detecting high-uncertainty tokens and injecting guided nudges based on reward model gradients. Rather than sampling multiple completions and ranking them as in Best-of-N approaches, GGRO monitors token-level entropy during decoding to identify regions of drift or misalignment, then uses gradient signals from a reward model to generate corrective tokens that redirect the generation trajectory. The method is designed to be lightweight and minimal-intervention, avoiding the computational costs of sampling-intensive search while circumventing the reward-hacking vulnerabilities of imperfect reward models through active steering rather than post-hoc filtering.
connects: This work extends the line of inference-time alignment techniques that adapt model behavior without retraining, building on prior methods like Best-of-N sampling and rejection sampling that rely on reward-guided search. It responds to fundamental limitations of these ranking-based approaches: their dependence on base model generation quality and susceptibility to reward model misspecification. The paper connects to recent work on uncertainty quantification in neural networks, entropy-based confidence estimation, and gradient-based guidance for controllable generation. It also relates to broader literature on distribution shift adaptation, test-time augmentation, and the challenge of alignment under out-of-distribution conditions that motivates inference-time interventions as a complementary strategy to RLHF and fine-tuning.
enables: GGRO opens directions for more efficient and robust alignment mechanisms that operate during generation rather than requiring expensive sampling or post-hoc filtering. The gradient-guidance framework could extend to other reward signals beyond safety and helpfulness, enabling fine-grained control over model behavior. The entropy-monitoring approach for detecting misalignment regions suggests future work on better uncertainty quantification and adaptive intervention strategies that only activate when needed. This work also motivates research into more reliable reward models that resist gradient-based exploitation, and hybrid approaches combining minimal-intervention steering with other alignment techniques. The method could facilitate deployment of LLMs in high-stakes settings where inference-time adaptation to distribution drift is critical for maintaining alignment.
